does: This paper mechanistically analyzes how RLHF affects partisan political orientation in Llama 3.1 8B by comparing internal representations before and after alignment training. Using sparse autoencoder decomposition, the authors demonstrate that RLHF does not eliminate the underlying partisan structure in the base model but rather suppresses its variance, producing behaviorally neutral outputs while preserving the latent partisan direction. They identify policy-encoding features that become completely inactive post-RLHF and use feature-level steering experiments to establish the causal relationship between these features and partisan behavior, revealing a functional compliance mechanism rather than fundamental value realignment.
connects: This work builds on growing evidence that alignment training produces only shallow behavioral compliance rather than deep value alignment, extending prior critiques of RLHF's opacity and effectiveness. It connects to mechanistic interpretability research using sparse autoencoders to understand model internals, similar to recent work probing learned representations. The paper engages with questions about whose values RLHF encodes and whether it genuinely removes problematic behaviors or merely masks them, building on concerns raised in alignment safety literature about the sufficiency of RLHF for creating robust, trustworthy systems. It also relates to research on value specification and the challenge of encoding human values in language model training.
enables: This work opens investigations into whether RLHF-induced behavioral suppression remains stable under distribution shift, adversarial prompting, or fine-tuning, with implications for deployed model safety. It suggests future mechanistic studies could examine how RLHF handles other value domains beyond politics—fairness, deception, harmful content—to assess whether the shallow-alignment pattern generalizes. The findings point toward developing alignment methods that achieve deep structural change rather than variance suppression, potentially through different training objectives or architectural modifications. Additionally, the sparse autoencoder approach could enable targeted steering experiments revealing which specific learned features control aligned versus unaligned behavior.
